1: King Belshazzar made a great feast for a thousand of his lords and drank wine in front of the thousand. The feast as the setting of hubris — Belshazzar celebrates imperial power. The Last Supper is the anti-feast: not a display of imperial abundance but bread broken and cup poured as symbols of the body given and blood shed (Luke 22:19-20). In one feast, vessels are desecrated; in the other, the vessel of Christ's body is voluntarily broken for others.
2: Belshazzar commanded that the vessels of gold and silver that Nebuchadnezzar his father had taken out of the temple in Jerusalem be brought, that the king and his lords might drink from them. The sacred vessels now used for imperial feasting — the holy brought into the service of the profane. Paul's warning about eating the Lord's Supper in an unworthy manner (1 Corinthians 11:27-29: whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup in an unworthy manner eats and drinks judgment on himself) echoes Belshazzar's profanation: the sacred cannot be treated as ordinary without consequence.
3: They brought in the golden vessels that had been taken out of the temple, the house of God in Jerusalem, and the king and his lords drank from them. The vessels consecrated for YHWH's service used for Babylonian imperial celebration — the inversion of holiness. Christ cleansed the temple of those who had turned the house of prayer into a den of robbers (Matthew 21:13) — the same pattern of sacred space and objects profaned for self-serving use.
4: They drank wine and praised the gods of gold and silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone. While drinking from the temple vessels, they praised the idol-gods — a double profanation: using holy vessels to toast false gods. Paul's description of idol-worship (1 Corinthians 10:21: you cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons) captures the same incompatibility. The vessels are holy; their repurposing for idol-service is the clearest possible act of covenant violation.
5: Immediately the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the plaster of the wall. The divine intrusion into the feast — the hand appears in the moment of maximum blasphemy. Christ interrupting the Pharisees' religious self-satisfaction with a word that exposes their condition (Matthew 23:27-28 — whitewashed tombs) is the same divine intrusion into human self-congratulation. The feast that seemed to go well becomes the scene of divine judgment.
6: The king's color changed, and his thoughts alarmed him; his limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together. The physical response to divine presence — the same dissolution of human confidence that appears before every theophany. Nebuchadnezzar's terrified prostration at the voice from heaven (Daniel 4:31-33) parallels the disciples falling on their faces at the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:6). Encounter with the divine reduces human self-sufficiency to trembling.
7: The king called loudly to bring in the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the astrologers. The same helpless turn to imperial wisdom that failed in chapters 2 and 4 — the pattern of Babylon reaching for its own resources first. The church's pattern is the opposite: the disciples immediately asked Jesus to interpret the parable (Mark 4:10); true disciples go to the living Word rather than the imperial wisdom-system for interpretation of divine disclosure.
8: Then all the king's wise men came in, but they could not read the writing or make known to the king the interpretation. The failure of Babylon's wisdom system is total and immediate — the writing on the wall defeats all human interpretive capacity. The resurrection, like the writing, exceeds human categories: the disciples' initial inability to understand (Luke 24:25-27 — O foolish ones, slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken) required divine interpretation from Christ himself.
9: King Belshazzar was greatly alarmed, and his color changed, and his lords were perplexed. The contagion of fear — the king's terror spreading to his entire court. The disciples in the storm-tossed boat (Mark 4:38-41) went from perplexity (do you not care that we perish?) to greater awe (who then is this?). Encounter with the divine, whether as terrifying judgment or as awe-inspiring mercy, produces perplexity that resolves only when the divine word speaks.
10: The queen, because of the words of the king and his lords, came into the banqueting hall. There is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods. The queen mother's memory spans two reigns — she alone remembers Daniel from Nebuchadnezzar's court. In every generation, there are those who remember what God has done and who point to the servants of the Spirit when the official wisdom fails. The church's memory of Christ's resurrection is this same witness: we have seen the Lord (John 20:25).
11: In the days of your father, light and understanding and wisdom like the wisdom of the gods were found in him. The queen's testimony to Daniel's Spirit-given wisdom preserves the memory across the generations. Paul's prayer for the Ephesians (Ephesians 1:17-18: the Spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him, having the eyes of your hearts enlightened) describes the same divine light that the queen attributes to Daniel — the Spirit-given illumination that no human system can replicate.
12: Because an excellent spirit, knowledge, and understanding to interpret dreams, explain riddles, and solve problems were found in this Daniel. The triple description of Daniel's gifts — excellent spirit, knowledge, understanding — corresponds to the threefold gift of the Spirit described in Isaiah's Servant passage (Isaiah 11:2: the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of knowledge and the fear of the LORD). All such wisdom-gifts are ultimately grounded in the one on whom the Spirit rests without measure (John 3:34).
13: Then Daniel was brought in before the king. Daniel's arrival at the feast he did not attend — summoned from outside the court scene to interpret what no insider could read. Christ's arrival into a world that did not know him (John 1:10-11: he was in the world and the world was made through him, yet the world did not know him) is the same pattern: the interpreter of divine reality comes from outside the human system that needs interpretation.
14: I have heard of you that the spirit of the gods is in you and that light and understanding and excellent wisdom are found in you. The king's recognition of divine wisdom in Daniel prefigures Pilate's recognition that Jesus had done nothing wrong — even pagan authority can perceive genuine divine quality without fully knowing what to do with it. Recognition without submission is the tragedy of both Belshazzar and Pilate.
15: The wise men and enchanters have been brought in before me to read this writing and make known to me its interpretation, but they could not show the interpretation of the matter. The repeated failure of human wisdom is emphasized before Daniel speaks — ensuring that when the interpretation comes, its source is unmistakably divine. Christ often let the failure of human solutions become evident before his own — the disciples' inability to cast out the demon (Mark 9:18), the disciples' inability to feed the crowd (John 6:9). The inadequacy of the available is the context for the sufficiency of the divine.
16: I have heard that you can give interpretations and solve problems. Now if you can read the writing and make known to me its interpretation, you shall be clothed in purple and have a chain of gold around your neck. The royal reward for interpretation — purple, gold chain, third ruler. Daniel will decline the personal benefit (Daniel 5:17) while still providing the interpretation. The servant who speaks the true word is not motivated by personal reward — a pattern Christ himself embodies (John 5:41: I do not receive glory from people).
17: Let your gifts be for yourself and give your rewards to another. Nevertheless, I will read the writing to the king and make known to him the interpretation. Daniel's refusal of reward before speaking truth — the uncorrupted prophet who cannot be bought. The contrast with the false prophets who prophesy for hire (Micah 3:11; Jeremiah 6:13) is stark. Christ taught freely: freely you received, freely give (Matthew 10:8). Truth-speaking must be uncorrupted by personal benefit to be trustworthy.
18: O king, the Most High God gave Nebuchadnezzar your father kingship and greatness and glory and majesty. Daniel's interpretation begins by establishing the theological history — what YHWH gave and what Nebuchadnezzar did with it. The sermon before the verdict: Christ's parables similarly establish the full picture before the judgment (Matthew 21:33-43 — the landowner's repeated overtures before the final coming of his son). Judgment is proportionate to revelation received.
19: And because of the greatness that he gave him, all peoples, nations, and languages trembled and feared before him. Whom he would, he killed, and whom he would, he kept alive. The absolute power of the empire over life and death — the power that belongs ultimately to God alone (Deuteronomy 32:39: I kill and I make alive). Christ claims this same power over life: I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25); I give them eternal life (John 10:28). The power of life and death is now located in the risen Son.
20: But when his heart was lifted up and his spirit was hardened so that he acted proudly, he was brought down from his kingly throne. The mechanism of the fall: heart lifted up, spirit hardened, pride acted out — then brought down. James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5: God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. The divine opposition to pride is not arbitrary but structural — pride is the refusal of the creature's dependence on the Creator, and God constitutionally resists it.
21: He was driven from among the sons of men, and his mind was made like that of a beast, and his dwelling was with the wild donkeys. He was fed grass like an ox. The humiliation rehearsed before Belshazzar — who knew all this (Daniel 5:22) — makes his pride inexcusable. The purpose of remembered history is to prevent the repetition of its failures. The church's proclamation of Christ crucified is the remembered event that prevents the pride-trap: the cross shows what human self-sufficiency produces and what divine grace supplies.
22: And you his son, Belshazzar, have not humbled your heart, though you knew all this. The indictment: knowledge without submission is worse than ignorance. Jesus's woes on the cities that witnessed his mighty works but did not repent (Matthew 11:20-24: then he began to denounce the cities where most of his mighty works had been done, because they did not repent) is the same principle: greater revelation creates greater accountability.
23: But you have lifted yourself up against the Lord of heaven. And the vessels of his house have been brought in before you, and you and your lords and your wives have drunk wine from them. And you have praised the gods of silver and gold. The triple indictment: pride against heaven, profanation of the sacred, and idol-worship. Paul's description of the pagan condition in Romans 1:21-23 matches exactly: they became futile in their thinking and exchanged the glory of the immortal God for images. Belshazzar is the concentrated case study of the fall described in Romans 1.
24: Then from his presence the hand was sent, and this writing was inscribed. The divine hand that writes judgment is YHWH acting directly in the banqueting hall of empire. Christ writing on the ground (John 8:6-8) while the accusers press for judgment is the same divine-hand-writing image — but in that case the writing diffuses judgment rather than announcing it. The same finger of God writes both condemnation and grace.
25: And this is the writing that was inscribed: Mene, Mene, Tekel, and Parsin. The three Aramaic terms — mene (numbered), tekel (weighed), parsin (divided) — are also the names of weights and monetary units. The divine accounting uses the empire's own commercial language: Babylon is measured in its own system of value and found insufficient.
26: Mene — God has numbered the days of your kingdom and brought it to an end. The divine numbering of the king's days: all human reigns are numbered, bounded by the decree of heaven. Christ's statement that no one knows the day or hour (Matthew 24:36) applies to the parousia; but every earthly kingdom's day and hour is known to the one who sets kings up and brings them down. Time itself is in the hands of the Ancient of Days.
27: Tekel — you have been weighed in the balances and found wanting. The weight-test metaphor: the scales of divine justice applied to the king's life and reign. 2 Corinthians 5:10 — we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body. Every human life will be weighed; only those in Christ who are clothed with his righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21) pass the weigh-in — not by their own merit but by his imputed weight of righteousness.
28: Peres — your kingdom is divided and given to the Medes and the Persians. The word peres also contains the wordplay on Paras (Persia) — the punishing power is linguistically embedded in the verdict. The empire that seemed permanent is divided and distributed to its successor in a single night. Christ's parable of the talents: from him who has not, even what he has will be taken (Matthew 25:29). What is not used faithfully will be reassigned.
29: Then Belshazzar commanded, and Daniel was clothed in purple, a chain of gold was put around his neck, and a proclamation was made about him, that he should be the third ruler in the kingdom. The reward given despite Daniel's earlier refusal — and despite the verdict of imminent doom. The empire's symbols (purple, gold, honor) bestowed in the hour of its collapse. The church received its commission (Matthew 28:18-20: all authority has been given to me — go therefore) in the aftermath of the cross, which looked like failure but was the hour of maximum divine authority.
30: That very night Belshazzar the Chaldean king was killed. The fulfillment of the verdict in the same night — the swift execution of divine judgment on the empire that profaned what was holy. The verse that ends Babylon's story is one of the Bible's most compressed: the world-empire from Nebuchadnezzar to Belshazzar ends in a single sentence. Christ's warning that this generation will not pass away until all these things take place (Mark 13:30) has the same compressed certainty: divine decrees execute on schedule.
31: And Darius the Mede received the kingdom, being about sixty-two years old. The transfer of empire — Babylon to Medo-Persia — in a single clause. The kingdoms of the world pass; only the kingdom of God endures (Daniel 2:44; Revelation 11:15). The stone cut without hands that struck Belshazzar's empire at its feast is the same stone that becomes the great mountain filling the whole earth — Christ's kingdom supplanting every human empire, not in a single night but through the slow transformation of the gospel moving across every nation, tribe, and tongue.
